name: Scholar's Mate

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Bc4 Nc6 3. Qh5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Bc4 Nc6 3. Qh5 g6 4. Qf3 Nf6
refutation_note: 3...g6 defends f7 and chases the queen. White's early queen sortie wastes tempo.
recognition_tip: When White brings out the queen to h5 early, your f7 pawn is the target. Defend it with g6 before developing the knight.

name: Scholar's Mate (Qf3 line)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Bc4 Nc6 3. Qf3

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Bc4 Nc6 3. Qf3 Nf6
refutation_note: 3...Nf6 defends f7 and develops a piece. The queen is misplaced on f3.
recognition_tip: When White plays Qf3 targeting f7, develop Nf6 to block the attack while gaining tempo.

name: Fool's Mate
refutation_note: Don't play f3 and g4 — these moves fatally weaken the king's diagonal. Play e4 instead.
recognition_tip: If you've played f3, never follow with g4. The h4-e1 diagonal is wide open for the queen.

name: Fried Liver Attack

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5
1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5 Na5
refutation_note: 5...Na5 attacks the bishop and avoids the Nxf7 sacrifice. Black wins the bishop pair and equalizes.
recognition_tip: When White plays Ng5 early against your Two Knights setup, don't recapture on d5 with the knight — it walks into Nxf7.

name: Fried Liver (5...b5 trap)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5 Na5
refutation_note: 5...b5 tries to trap the bishop but fails — play 5...Na5 instead, which is theoretically sound.
recognition_tip: Don't try to trap the c4 bishop with b5 — White can simply retreat. Focus on counterplay with Na5.

name: Légal's Mate

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. Bc4 Bg4 4. Nc3 g6 5. Nxe5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. Bc4 Bg4 4. Nc3 g6 5. Nxe5 dxe5
refutation_note: Don't capture the queen! Take back on e5 instead — the queen sacrifice is a trap leading to checkmate.
recognition_tip: When a knight takes on e5 and it looks like a free queen, check for Bxf7+ and Nd5# mating patterns first.

name: Stafford Gambit (Oh no my queen)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nc6 4. Nxc6 dxc6
1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nc6 4. Nxc6 dxc6 5. d3 Bc5 6. Bg5 Nxe4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nc6 4. Nxc6 dxc6 5. d3 Bc5 6. Be2
refutation_note: Avoid Bg5 — it walks into the tactical sequence. Develop calmly with Be2 and castle quickly.
recognition_tip: In the Stafford Gambit, if you take on d8 after Nxe4, Black has Bxf2+ and Bg4# checkmate.

name: Stafford Gambit (Ng4 attack)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nc6 4. Nxc6 dxc6 5. d3 Bc5 6. Be2 h5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nc6 4. Nxc6 dxc6 5. d3 Bc5 6. Be2 h5 7. Bg5
refutation_note: Don't castle into h5/Ng4 attack. Play Bg5 to pin the knight and neutralize Black's kingside threats.
recognition_tip: When Black plays h5 in the Stafford Gambit, they're preparing Ng4 with threats on h2. Don't castle kingside yet.

name: Englund Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 e5 2. dxe5 Nc6 3. Nf3 Qe7 4. Bf4 Qb4+
1. d4 e5 2. dxe5 Nc6 3. Nf3 Qe7 4. Bf4 Qb4+ 5. Bd2

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 e5 2. dxe5 Nc6 3. Nf3 Qe7 4. Nc3
refutation_note: Don't play Bf4 — it allows Qb4+. Play Nc3 developing naturally and holding e5.
recognition_tip: After 1.d4 e5 2.dxe5 Nc6 3.Nf3 Qe7, watch out for Qb4+ check — develop with Nc3 instead of Bf4.

name: Budapest Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 Ng4
1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 Ng4 4. Bf4 Nc6 5. Nf3 Bb4+ 6. Nbd2 Qe7 7. a3 Ngxe5 8. Nxe5 Nxe5

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 Ng4 4. Bf4 Nc6 5. Nf3 Bb4+ 6. Nc3
refutation_note: Block with Nc3 instead of Nbd2. The Nd3# smothered mate pattern only works when d2 is occupied by a knight.
recognition_tip: In the Budapest Gambit, if Black plays Bb4+ with a knight on g4, block with Nc3, not Nbd2 — watch for smothered mate on d3.

name: Elephant Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 Nbd7 5. cxd5 exd5 6. Nxd5

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 Nbd7 5. cxd5 exd5 6. e3
refutation_note: Don't grab the d5 pawn with the knight — after Nxd5 Bxd8 Bb4+ you lose the queen. Play e3 developing naturally.
recognition_tip: In the QGD with Bg5 and Nbd7, taking on d5 with the knight allows Bb4+ winning the queen after Bxd8.

name: Noah's Ark Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 d6 5. d4 b5 6. Bb3 exd4
1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 d6 5. d4 b5 6. Bb3 exd4 7. Nxd4 Nxd4 8. Qxd4 c5 9. Qd5 Be6 10. Qc6+ Bd7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 d6 5. d4 b5 6. Bb3 exd4 7. Nxd4 Nxd4 8. Qxd4 c5 9. Qd3
refutation_note: Retreat the queen to d3 to keep the bishop safe — the c4 pawn advance traps the bishop on b3 with no escape.
recognition_tip: In the Ruy Lopez, watch for ...a6, ...b5, ...c5, ...c4 trapping your bishop on b3. Keep an escape route open.

name: Lasker Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 d4 4. e3 Bb4+ 5. Bd2 dxe3

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 d4 4. e3 Bb4+ 5. Bd2 dxe3 6. fxe3
refutation_note: Take with the f-pawn (fxe3) instead of Bxb4. After Bxb4 exf2+ Ke2 fxg1=N+ forks the king and rook.
recognition_tip: In the Albin Countergambit after dxe3, don't take the bishop — the underpromotion to a knight with check wins material.

name: Fishing Pole Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. O-O Ng4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. O-O Ng4 5. d4
refutation_note: Don't play h3 — it walks into h5 and a devastating kingside attack. Play d4 gaining center space and opening lines.
recognition_tip: When Black plays Ng4 in the Ruy Lopez, don't push h3 — it opens h-file attacking lines after ...h5.

name: Blackburne-Shilling Gambit

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nd4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nd4 4. Nxd4 exd4 5. O-O
refutation_note: Take the knight on d4 instead of grabbing e5. After Nxe5 Qg5, White faces a deadly attack.
recognition_tip: If Black plays Nd4 in the Italian, don't take e5 — take the knight! The 'free' e5 pawn is a trap.

name: Caro-Kann Smothered Mate

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c6 2. d4 d5 3. Nc3 dxe4 4. Nxe4 Nd7 5. Qe2

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c6 2. d4 d5 3. Nc3 dxe4 4. Nxe4 Nd7 5. Qe2 Ndf6
refutation_note: Develop with Ndf6 (from d7), not Ngf6 (from g8). Ngf6 blocks the king's escape and allows Nd6#.
recognition_tip: In the Caro-Kann after Nd7, if White plays Qe2, develop the d7-knight to f6, not the g8-knight. The g8-knight on f6 blocks your king.

name: Petroff Defense Piece Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 d6 4. Nf3 Nxe4
refutation_note: Play 3...d6 first to kick the knight, then recapture on e4. Taking immediately with 3...Nxe4 allows Qe2 with discovered attack.
recognition_tip: In the Petroff, don't grab e4 immediately — first play d6 to force the knight back, then take on e4 safely.

name: Greek Gift Sacrifice (Bxh7+)

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Bf4 e6 4. e3 Be7 5. Bd3 O-O 6. Bxh7+

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Bf4 e6 4. e3 Be7 5. Bd3 O-O 6. Bxh7+ Kh8
refutation_note: Decline the sacrifice with Kh8! Taking with Kxh7 allows Ng5+ and a devastating attack. After Kh8, White has sacrificed a bishop for nothing.
recognition_tip: When a bishop takes on h7 with check, consider declining with Kh8. The classic Greek Gift only works if you take the bishop.

name: Mortimer Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. d3 Ne7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 Nf6 4. d3 Ne7 5. O-O
refutation_note: Don't take e5 — after c6 and Qa5+, the knight on e5 and bishop are both attacked. Castle instead.
recognition_tip: When Black plays Ne7 in the Ruy Lopez, the e5 pawn looks free but c6/Qa5+ wins material back with interest.

name: Siberian Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Nf3 c6 5. Bg5 dxc4

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Nf3 c6 5. Bg5 dxc4 6. a4
refutation_note: Play a4 to prevent b5 and recover the c4 pawn. The e4 push allows Black to hold the pawn with b5 and create complications.
recognition_tip: In the Slav Defense after dxc4, don't rush e4 — Black can hold the pawn with b5 and create a dangerous counterattack.

name: Latvian Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 f5 3. Nxe5 Qf6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 f5 3. Nxe5 Qf6 4. Nc4 fxe4 5. d3
refutation_note: Retreat the knight to c4 rather than playing d4. Keep things simple and the extra pawn advantage.
recognition_tip: After the Latvian Gambit's Qf6, retreat the knight calmly. Don't overextend in the center.

name: Elephant Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d5 3. exd5 e4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d5 3. exd5 e4 4. Nd4
refutation_note: Jump the knight to d4 instead of Qe2. The knight is well-placed and you keep the extra pawn.
recognition_tip: Against the Elephant Gambit, don't block your bishop with Qe2. Develop naturally and hold your extra pawn.

name: Albin Countergambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 d4

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e5 3. dxe5 d4 4. Nf3 Nc6 5. Nbd2
refutation_note: Develop with Nf3 instead of e3. The e3 move walks into the Lasker Trap with the underpromotion knight fork.
recognition_tip: In the Albin Countergambit after d4, don't play e3 — develop pieces naturally with Nf3.

name: Italian Game Fork Trick

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Nc3 Nxe4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Nc3 Nxe4 5. Bxf7+ Ke7 6. d4
refutation_note: After 4...Nxe4, play 5.Bxf7+ instead of 5.Nxe4, which allows d5 forking knight and bishop.
recognition_tip: When Black takes on e4 in the Italian, don't recapture with the knight — d5 forks your pieces. Try Bxf7+ instead.

name: QGD Cambridge Springs Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 Nbd7 5. Nf3 c6 6. e3 Qa5

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 Nbd7 5. Nf3 c6 6. e3 Qa5 7. Bxf6 Nxf6 8. Bd3
refutation_note: Exchange the bishop for the knight to remove the pin threats. Nd2 walks into Bb4 pinning the knight to the queen.
recognition_tip: In the Cambridge Springs variation, Black's Qa5 creates threats along the a5-d2 diagonal. Don't play Nd2 — the knight gets pinned.

name: Danish Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. d4 exd4 3. c3 dxc3 4. Bc4 cxb2 5. Bxb2

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. d4 exd4 3. c3 dxc3 4. Bc4 cxb2 5. Bxb2 d6
refutation_note: Play d6 (not d5) to solidify the position. After d5, White gets a powerful attack with Bxd5 and Bxf7+.
recognition_tip: In the Danish Gambit, don't play d5 trying to return material — White's bishops rip open your position. Play d6 and develop.

name: Scotch Game Knight Fork

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. d4 exd4 4. Nxd4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. d4 exd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3
refutation_note: Don't trade knights — develop with Nf6 instead. After Nxd4 Qxd4, White has a strong centralized queen.
recognition_tip: In the Scotch Game, don't exchange on d4 — it gives White a powerful central queen. Play Nf6 developing with tempo.

name: Halloween Gambit

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Nxe5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Nxe5 Nxe5 5. d4 Ng6 6. e5 Ng8 7. Bc4 d5
refutation_note: After taking on e5, retreat to g6 (not c6). This avoids d5 kicking the knight and keeps material advantage.
recognition_tip: Against the Halloween Gambit (Nxe5 sacrifice), accept the knight but retreat to g6 to avoid getting pushed around.

name: Tennison Gambit (Scholar's Mate reversed)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 d5 2. Nf3 dxe4 3. Ng5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 d5 2. Nf3 dxe4 3. Ng5 f5 4. d3 exd3 5. Bxd3
refutation_note: Play f5 to defend the e4 pawn and block the Bc4 threat to f7. Nf6 allows Bc4 targeting your f7 pawn.
recognition_tip: After Ng5 targeting f7, defend with f5 to hold the extra pawn rather than developing the knight.

name: King's Gambit Muzio Gambit

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. f4 exf4 3. Nf3 g5 4. Bc4 g4 5. O-O

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. f4 exf4 3. Nf3 g5 4. Bc4 g4 5. O-O d5 6. exd5 gxf3
refutation_note: Play d5! Open the center before taking the knight. This neutralizes White's attacking setup.
recognition_tip: In the Muzio Gambit, White sacrifices the knight for a massive attack. Counter with d5 to open the center and activate your pieces.

name: Smith-Morra Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. d4 cxd4 3. c3 dxc3 4. Nxc3 Nc6 5. Nf3 d6 6. Bc4 e6 7. O-O Nf6 8. Qe2 Be7 9. Rd1

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. d4 cxd4 3. c3 dxc3 4. Nxc3 Nc6 5. Nf3 d6 6. Bc4 e6 7. O-O Nf6 8. Qe2 Be7 9. Rd1 O-O
refutation_note: Castle first instead of playing e5. The e5 push allows Nb5 hitting d6 and threatening forks.
recognition_tip: In the Smith-Morra, don't push e5 too early — it weakens d5 and d6. Complete development first.

name: Wayward Queen Attack (Parham)

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Qh5 Nc6 3. Bc4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Qh5 Nc6 3. Bc4 g6 4. Qf3 Nf6
refutation_note: Play g6 to defend f7 before developing Nf6. The queen on h5 targets f7 — neutralize the threat first.
recognition_tip: Against the Wayward Queen (2.Qh5), always defend f7 before developing the knight to f6.

name: Benoni Fork Trick

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 e6 4. Nc3 exd5 5. cxd5 d6 6. Nf3 g6 7. Nd2 Nbd7 8. Nc4 Nb6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 e6 4. Nc3 exd5 5. cxd5 d6 6. Nf3 g6 7. Nd2 Nbd7 8. Nc4 Nb6 9. Ne3
refutation_note: Retreat the knight to e3 — don't exchange on b6 which opens the a-file for Black's rook.
recognition_tip: In the Benoni, exchanging Nxb6 opens the a-file and gives Black powerful counterplay. Keep the knight active.

name: Frankenstein-Dracula Variation

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nc3 Nf6 3. Bc4 Nxe4 4. Qh5 Nd6 5. Bb3 Nc6
1. e4 e5 2. Nc3 Nf6 3. Bc4 Nxe4 4. Qh5 Nd6 5. Bb3 Nc6 6. Nb5 g6 7. Qf3 f5 8. Qd5 Qe7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nc3 Nf6 3. Bc4 Nxe4 4. Qh5 Nd6 5. Bb3 Nc6 6. Nb5 g6 7. Qf3 f5 8. Qd5 Qe7 9. Nf3
refutation_note: Develop with Nf3 instead of grabbing the rook. The knight on a8 gets trapped and Black's pieces are very active.
recognition_tip: Don't grab the rook on a8 — the knight gets trapped in the corner while Black's pieces swarm your king.

name: Philidor Defense Legal Variation

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 Bg4 4. dxe5 Bxf3 5. Qxf3 dxe5 6. Bc4 Nf6 7. Qb3

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 Bg4 4. dxe5 Bxf3 5. Qxf3 dxe5 6. Bc4 Nf6 7. Qb3 Qd7
refutation_note: Play Qd7 defending f7 and b7 simultaneously. Qe7 blocks the bishop and leaves f7 weak.
recognition_tip: When White plays Qb3 targeting b7 and f7, respond with Qd7 to defend both squares at once.

name: Traxler Counterattack

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 Bc5
1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 Bc5 5. Nxf7 Bxf2+

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 Bc5 5. Nxf7 Bxf2+ 6. Kf1
refutation_note: Decline with Kf1 — taking with Kxf2 allows a vicious attack with d5 and Ng4+.
recognition_tip: After Bc5 Nxf7 Bxf2+, do NOT take the bishop. Kf1 keeps the king safer.

name: Evans Gambit Declined Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Bc5 4. b4 Bb6 5. a4 a6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Bc5 4. b4 Bb6 5. a4 a5 6. b5 Nd4
refutation_note: After a5, White plays a5 trapping the bishop on b6. Black should play Nf6 or d6 instead.
recognition_tip: In the Evans Gambit declined, watch for a4 — if you play a6, the a5 pawn push traps your bishop.

name: Sicilian Magnus Smith Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 Nc6 6. Bc4 g6
1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 Nc6 6. Bc4 g6 7. Nxc6 bxc6 8. e5 dxe5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 Nc6 6. Bc4 e6
refutation_note: Play e6 instead of g6 to guard f7 and avoid the Bxf7+ sacrifice.
recognition_tip: In the Sicilian with Bc4, playing g6 can be dangerous — e5/Bxf7+ wins the queen after Kxf7 Qxd8.

name: French Winawer Poisoned Pawn

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e6 2. d4 d5 3. Nc3 Bb4 4. e5 c5 5. a3 Bxc3+ 6. bxc3 Ne7 7. Qg4 Qc7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e6 2. d4 d5 3. Nc3 Bb4 4. e5 c5 5. a3 Bxc3+ 6. bxc3 Ne7 7. Qg4 O-O
refutation_note: Don't grab the g7 pawn — Black gets Rg8 trapping the queen. Castle instead.
recognition_tip: In the Winawer French, the g7 pawn is poisoned — Qxg7 gets trapped by Rg8.

name: Caro-Kann Advance Variation Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c6 2. d4 d5 3. e5 Bf5 4. Nc3 e6 5. g4 Bg6 6. Nge2 c5 7. h4 h5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c6 2. d4 d5 3. e5 Bf5 4. Nc3 e6 5. g4 Bg6 6. Nge2 c5 7. h4 h6
refutation_note: Don't allow Nf4 — play h6 to prevent the knight landing with a strong attack.
recognition_tip: In the Caro-Kann advance with g4, watch for Nf5 landing with devastating effect.

name: Scandinavian Icelandic Gambit

positions.pgn: 1. e4 d5 2. exd5 Nf6 3. c4 e6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 d5 2. exd5 Nf6 3. c4 e6 4. d4
refutation_note: Don't take on e6 — Black gets rapid development with Bxe6 targeting c4 and quick castling. Hold the center with d4.
recognition_tip: In the Scandinavian with c4, taking dxe6 opens lines for Black's pieces too quickly.

name: Dutch Staunton Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 f5 2. e4 fxe4 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 Nc6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 f5 2. e4 fxe4 3. Nc3 Nf6 4. Bg5 e6
refutation_note: Don't push d5 — it allows Bxf6 followed by Bb5. Play e6 solidifying the position.
recognition_tip: Against the Staunton Gambit with Bg5, don't weaken your queenside with d5.

name: Fried Liver Na5 Counter-Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5 Na5 6. Bb5+

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. Ng5 d5 5. exd5 Na5 6. Bb5+ Bd7
refutation_note: Block with Bd7 instead of c6 — after c6 dxc6 bxc6 the bishop on b5 is harassed but White keeps the extra pawn.
recognition_tip: After Na5 Bb5+, block with Bd7. Playing c6 weakens your pawn structure.

name: Sicilian Najdorf Pin Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 a6 6. Bg5 e6 7. f4 Qb6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 a6 6. Bg5 e6 7. f4 Qb6 8. Qd2
refutation_note: After Qb6, don't play Nb3 — Black wins a pawn with Qe3+. Play Qd2 defending b2 and keeping the knight active.
recognition_tip: In the Najdorf with Bg5, if Black plays Qb6, Nb3 drops the e4 pawn to Qe3+.

name: Queen's Gambit Accepted Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 dxc4 3. e3 b5

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 dxc4 3. e3 b5 4. a4 c6 5. axb5 cxb5 6. Qf3
refutation_note: After a4, Black should play c6 to hold the pawn chain. But White's Qf3 hits the rook on a8.
recognition_tip: In the QGA, trying to hold the c4 pawn with b5 often fails to a4 undermining the chain.

name: King's Indian Bayonet Attack Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 g6 3. Nc3 Bg7 4. e4 d6 5. Nf3 O-O 6. Be2 e5 7. O-O Nc6 8. d5 Ne7 9. b4 Nh5 10. Re1

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 g6 3. Nc3 Bg7 4. e4 d6 5. Nf3 O-O 6. Be2 e5 7. O-O Nc6 8. d5 Ne7 9. b4 a5
refutation_note: Challenge the bayonet with a5 on the queenside instead of premature f5.
recognition_tip: In the King's Indian Bayonet, premature f5 without preparation can backfire. Play a5 first.

name: Pirc Austrian Attack Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 d6 2. d4 Nf6 3. Nc3 g6 4. f4 Bg7 5. Nf3 O-O 6. e5 Nfd7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 d6 2. d4 Nf6 3. Nc3 g6 4. f4 Bg7 5. Nf3 O-O 6. e5 dxe5
refutation_note: Take immediately with dxe5 instead of retreating to d7. After Nfd7 e6, the pawn cramps Black fatally.
recognition_tip: In the Pirc Austrian Attack, don't let e5-e6 land — challenge it immediately.

name: Ruy Lopez Marshall Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 Nf6 5. O-O Be7 6. Re1 b5 7. Bb3 O-O 8. c3 d5 9. exd5 Nxd5 10. Nxe5 Nxe5 11. Rxe5 c6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 Nf6 5. O-O Be7 6. Re1 b5 7. Bb3 O-O 8. c3 d5 9. exd5 Nxd5 10. Nxe5 Nxe5 11. Rxe5 c6 12. Re1
refutation_note: Retreat the rook instead of playing d4. The Marshall Attack gives Black powerful attacking chances after d4.
recognition_tip: In the Marshall Attack, Black sacrifices a pawn for a massive kingside attack. Re1 is safer than d4.

name: Queen's Gambit Baltic Defense Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 Bf5 3. cxd5 Bxb1

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 Bf5 3. cxd5 Bxb1 4. Rxb1
refutation_note: Take the bishop with Rxb1 instead of Qa4+. After Qa4+ c6 dxc6 Qxc6, Black has equalized.
recognition_tip: In the Baltic Defense after Bxb1, simply recapture — don't get greedy with Qa4+.

name: London System Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 c5 3. e3 Nc6 4. c3 Qb6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 c5 3. e3 Nc6 4. c3 Qb6 5. Qc1
refutation_note: Don't play Qb3 exchanging queens — it leaves the b2 pawn backward and weak. Retreat Qc1 keeping the bishop protected.
recognition_tip: In the London, when Black plays Qb6 pressuring b2, don't trade queens — it damages your structure.

name: Catalan Pawn Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3. g3 d5 4. Bg2 dxc4 5. Nf3 Nc6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3. g3 d5 4. Bg2 dxc4 5. Nf3 a6
refutation_note: Play a6 to hold the c4 pawn instead of Nc6. After Nc6, White recovers the pawn with Qa4.
recognition_tip: In the Catalan, holding c4 with Nc6 fails to Qa4 forking knight and pawn.

name: Réti Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. Nf3 d5 2. c4 dxc4 3. e3 Nf6

refutation_pgn: 1. Nf3 d5 2. c4 dxc4 3. e3 b5
refutation_note: Hold the pawn with b5 — giving it back with Nf6 Bxc4 lets White develop quickly.
recognition_tip: In the Réti gambit, don't give back c4 easily — hold it with b5 or Be6.

name: Sicilian Dragon Exchange Sacrifice

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 g6 6. Be3 Bg7 7. f3 O-O 8. Qd2 Nc6 9. Bc4 Bd7 10. O-O-O Rc8

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 g6 6. Be3 Bg7 7. f3 O-O 8. Qd2 Nc6 9. Bc4 Bd7 10. O-O-O Ne5 11. Bb3 Rc8 12. h4
refutation_note: In the Yugoslav Attack, don't play Bb3 too early — start the h4-h5 pawn storm first.
recognition_tip: In the Dragon Yugoslav Attack, prioritize the h-pawn storm over retreating the bishop.

name: Nimzo-Indian Classical Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Bb4 4. Qc2 d5 5. cxd5 exd5 6. Bg5 h6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 e6 3. Nc3 Bb4 4. Qc2 d5 5. cxd5 exd5 6. Bg5 c5
refutation_note: After h6, retreat with Bxf6 rather than Bh4 — the bishop gets trapped after g5.
recognition_tip: In the Nimzo-Indian with Bg5, if Black plays h6, exchange rather than retreating to h4 where g5 traps it.

name: Benko Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 b5 4. cxb5 a6 5. bxa6 Bxa6

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 b5 4. cxb5 a6 5. bxa6 Bxa6 6. e4
refutation_note: Play e4 grabbing the center, not Nc3 which allows Black's powerful queenside pressure with d6/Nbd7/g6/Bg7.
recognition_tip: In the Benko Gambit, don't develop passively. Seize the center with e4 immediately.

name: Slav Exchange Variation Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 c6 3. cxd5 cxd5 4. Bf4 Nc6 5. e3 Nf6 6. Nc3 Bg4

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 d5 2. c4 c6 3. cxd5 cxd5 4. Bf4 Nc6 5. e3 Nf6 6. Nc3 a6
refutation_note: After Bg4, Qb3 forks b7 and d5. Play a6 to prevent Bb5 ideas and keep the position solid.
recognition_tip: In the Exchange Slav, developing with Bg4 can walk into Qb3 hitting b7 and d5.

name: Sicilian Alapin Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. c3 d5 3. exd5 Qxd5 4. d4 Nc6 5. Nf3 cxd4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 c5 2. c3 d5 3. exd5 Qxd5 4. d4 e6
refutation_note: Play e6 developing naturally. After cxd4 cxd4, White gets a strong isolated queen pawn.
recognition_tip: In the Alapin Sicilian, don't trade pawns on d4 too early — it helps White's development.

name: Benoni Taimanov Attack Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 e6 4. Nc3 exd5 5. cxd5 d6 6. e4 g6 7. f4 Bg7 8. Bb5+

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 c5 3. d5 e6 4. Nc3 exd5 5. cxd5 d6 6. e4 g6 7. f4 Bg7 8. Bb5+ Bd7
refutation_note: Block with Bd7 instead of Nfd7 — the knight retreating gives White too much central control with e5.
recognition_tip: Against Bb5+ in the Modern Benoni, block with Bd7 to keep the knight active on f6.

name: Grünfeld Exchange Variation Trap

positions.pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 g6 3. Nc3 d5 4. cxd5 Nxd5 5. e4 Nxc3 6. bxc3 Bg7 7. Bc4 c5 8. Ne2 O-O 9. O-O Nc6 10. Be3 Bg4

refutation_pgn: 1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 g6 3. Nc3 d5 4. cxd5 Nxd5 5. e4 Nxc3 6. bxc3 Bg7 7. Bc4 c5 8. Ne2 O-O 9. O-O Nc6 10. Be3 Bg4 11. Qd3
refutation_note: After Bg4, play Qd3 instead of f3 — chasing the bishop with f3 weakens the king and allows tactical shots.
recognition_tip: In the Grünfeld Exchange, f3 against Bg4 weakens the kingside. Prefer Qd3 keeping the structure intact.

name: Scotch Four Knights Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. d4 exd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 Bb4 6. Nxc6 bxc6 7. Bd3 d5 8. exd5 cxd5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. d4 exd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 Bb4 6. Nxc6 bxc6 7. Bd3 d5 8. exd5 cxd5 9. Qe2+
refutation_note: Play Qe2+ before castling — after O-O, Black has O-O with strong piece activity and initiative.
recognition_tip: In the Scotch with Bb4, check with Qe2+ to disrupt Black's development before castling.

name: King's Gambit Falkbeer Counter-Gambit Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. f4 d5 3. exd5 e4

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. f4 d5 3. exd5 e4 4. Nc3
refutation_note: Play Nc3 developing and pressuring e4 — d3 opens lines for Black's bishops dangerously.
recognition_tip: In the Falkbeer Counter-Gambit, don't play d3 returning the pawn — develop with Nc3 instead.

name: Vienna Game Copycat Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nc3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Bc5 4. Qg4 Qf6

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nc3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Bc5 4. Qg4 g6
refutation_note: Defend g7 with g6 or Kf8, don't play Qf6 which allows Nd5 forking queen and c7.
recognition_tip: In the Vienna Game, if White plays Qg4 threatening g7, don't mirror with Qf6 — Nd5 forks.

name: Philidor Defense Exchange Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 Nd7

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 d6 3. d4 exd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6
refutation_note: Exchange on d4 instead of blocking with Nd7. After Nd7, Bc4 targets f7 and Black is cramped.
recognition_tip: In the Philidor, don't block with Nd7 too early — exchange on d4 to relieve pressure on f7.

name: Max Lange Attack Trap

positions.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. d4 exd4 5. O-O Bc5 6. e5

refutation_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bc4 Nf6 4. d4 exd4 5. O-O Bc5 6. e5 Ng4
refutation_note: Play Ng4 keeping the knight active — d5 blocks the bishop and leads to a sharp attack for White after exf6.
recognition_tip: In the Max Lange Attack after e5, don't block with d5 — keep pieces active with Ng4.
